steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence has two independent clauses: (1) "many are still well [known]" and (2) "Koulsy Lamko, for example, is a celebrated playwright from Chad." The second clause exemplifies the first. The blank must provide punctuation that correctly joins two independent clauses where the second elaborates on the first.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — known: No punctuation between two independent clauses creates a run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice B — known:: A colon correctly joins two independent clauses when the second illustrates or explains the first. "For example" signals that Lamko exemplifies the claim about writers being well known — exactly the elaboration a colon signals. ✓
  • Choice C — known and: "And" is a coordinating conjunction that joins grammatically equal elements, but the second clause doesn't simply add a parallel idea — it gives a specific example. "And" also produces an awkward reading here. ✗
  • Choice D — known,: A comma alone between two independent clauses is a comma splice — a grammatical error. ✗
That matches Choice B — known:.
